name: Bachelor of Science in Criminology
overview: The BS Criminology program prepares students for careers in law enforcement, public safety, criminal investigation, and the justice system. It covers criminal law, forensic science, criminalistics, police administration, and correctional management. Graduates are eligible to take the Criminologist Licensure Examination.
ideal_student: This program suits students who are drawn to justice, public safety, and law enforcement. Students who are physically fit, disciplined, courageous, and motivated by protecting their community tend to thrive here. It is also ideal for students interested in forensic science, criminal investigation, and understanding why crimes happen and how to prevent them.
skills: Criminal investigation, forensic analysis, report writing, legal reasoning, physical fitness, discipline, crisis management, firearms handling, public safety procedures, and ethical judgment.
careers: Police officer (PNP), criminologist, forensic scientist, NBI agent, jail officer, fire officer, customs officer, private investigator, security manager, probation officer, criminal justice researcher.
work_environment: Graduates work in the Philippine National Police, NBI, Bureau of Jail Management, Bureau of Fire Protection, customs, immigration, private security firms, and research institutions. The work involves fieldwork, investigations, report preparation, and community safety operations.
strand_alignment: Recommended for students from HUMSS strand. Students from GAS, STEM, or TVL with discipline, physical fitness, and interest in law enforcement may also qualify.
keywords: criminology crime law enforcement police justice forensic investigation security public safety NBI PNP detective criminal law government protect community discipline order forensic science jail correctional officer safety defense legal investigation board exam licensure
holland_rationale: BSCRIM is primarily Realistic because law enforcement and criminal investigation involve direct physical action, fieldwork, practical procedural work, and operating in structured command environments. Social is secondary because criminology fundamentally serves community protection and justice, and practitioners regularly engage with victims, communities, and interdisciplinary justice teams.
